design_philosophy.core_loop: Select two companion characters with unique abilities, navigate the mansion, solve puzzles using character-specific mechanics, avoid hostile NPCs, rescue Sandy and escape
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Non-linear puzzle solving with character-dependent solutions; player must plan team composition carefully as wrong choices lock out entire puzzle paths; resource tension from limited character pool (if all die, game ends)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Comedic horror adventure—teenage heroes infiltrating a mad scientist's mansion, leveraging unique skills to overcome obstacles, with multiple valid approaches to victory
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Pioneered point-and-click interface as alternative to text-command adventure games; non-linear design where puzzle solutions are gated by character abilities rather than progression gates; multiple endings based on team composition and survival; rejects single-solution design in favor of emergent gameplay through character selection

difficulty.lives_system: Three-character roster; game ends if all selected characters are killed. Hostile mansion inhabitants throw players into dungeon or kill them upon detection
difficulty.checkpointing: Implicit through character death system; can continue with surviving characters
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High—player has complete agency through character selection at game start. Choosing certain character combinations locks out specific puzzle solutions, requiring different paths. Player decides which two of six companions to bring, creating variable difficulty curves

progression.unlock_mechanism: Character-dependent puzzle solutions. Each of six available characters (Dave, Bernard, Jeff, Michael, Razor, Syd, Wendy) possesses unique abilities (e.g., Syd and Razor play instruments, Bernard repairs appliances). Puzzles are only solvable by specific characters, forcing different routes through mansion based on team composition
replayability_hooks: Five possible endings based on character selection, survival, and accomplishments
Six characters with mutually exclusive abilities creating multiple distinct playthroughs
Non-linear puzzle design rewards experimentation with different character combinations
Early NES port required heavy reworking for Nintendo, suggesting alternate content or solutions exist

world_building_techniques: Cutscenes interrupt gameplay to advance story and convey offscreen events (Ron Gilbert coined the term 'cutscene' for this game)
Environmental storytelling through mansion layout and NPC presence (Dr. Fred—mad scientist, Nurse Edna, Weird Ed, two sentient tentacles)
Dialogue displayed above game scenes contextualizes puzzle objectives and character interactions
Backstory delivered via intro: sentient meteor crashed 20 years prior, brainwashed Edisons, commanded Dr. Fred to harvest human brains for experiments
Comedic tone grounded in B-movie horror clichés, undercutting narrative tension with humor

boss_design.philosophy: Not traditional boss encounters; instead, narrative-driven confrontation with Dr. Fred (controlled by sentient meteor) as final antagonist. Primary challenge is puzzle-solving to overcome mansion obstacles rather than combat encounters
notable_bosses: Dr. Fred Edison (mad scientist antagonist, mind enslaved by sentient meteor)
Mansion inhabitants as dynamic obstacles (Nurse Edna, Weird Ed, hostile NPCs who throw players into dungeon)

faction_indicators: Edison family (Dr. Fred, Nurse Edna, Weird Ed) as hostile occupants
Green tentacle as anomalous non-hostile entity (only friendly NPC in mansion)
Player characters represent teen outsider faction invading family territory
danger_signals: Mansion interior as dangerous territory—hostile NPCs actively seek to kill or imprison intruders
Sentient meteor as existential threat (brainwashing, forced experimentation)
Sandy Pantz as kidnapped victim requiring rescue
environmental_storytelling: Mansion layout reveals family dysfunction and mad science operations
Character-specific items and puzzle solutions embedded in environment
NPC patrol patterns and dialogue convey mansion as hostile controlled space

sources_used: Wikipedia: Maniac Mansion (overview, development, game mechanics, narrative, character abilities, endings system)
